meta.purpose: Key differentiators that predict callback success - trimmed of non-predictive patterns
meta.methodology: Removed patterns that also appeared in failed applications; retained only proven differentiators

factor.name: Tech Stack Front-Loading with Exact Match
factor.description: First 2-3 resume bullets use employer's EXACT technologies by name, not generic categories

success_examples.lawdepot: First bullet: "Architect backend services using C#, .NET 7+, Entity Framework Core" - matched their exact stack
success_examples.onfleet: Subtitle: "Mobile & Platform Development", summary: "5+ years hands-on mobile (iOS/Android), shipped 12+ apps"

failure_contrast.nextiva: AI/ML in subtitle but no modern AI stack (transformers, RAG) mentioned anywhere
failure_contrast.shakepay: Listed "Node.js, Python, SQL" generically without depth or recent proof

factor.implementation: Research their tech stack, put their exact tools/frameworks in first 3 bullets using their terminology

factor.name: Concrete Proof Over Claims
factor.description: Specific, verifiable achievements with numbers - not adjectives or vague expertise claims

success_examples.lawdepot: "95%+ on-time delivery", "99.5% reliability", "35% performance improvements", "90%+ code coverage", "1000+ automated scenarios daily"
success_examples.onfleet: "Shipped 12+ mobile apps", "500k+ downloads", "40% build time reduction", "95%+ on-time delivery"

failure_contrast.nextiva: "AI/ML Systems" claimed but only vague "MCP integration" without scale or metrics
failure_contrast.shakepay: "Build and ship production-level backend services" - no numbers, no scale, no specifics

factor.key_difference: Every major claim has a number attached. "Expertise" without proof = red flag

factor.name: Singular Focus in Positioning
factor.description: ONE clear value proposition in subtitle/summary that directly answers "What does this person do best?"

success_examples.lawdepot: Title: "Software Development Manager | Engineering Leader" - clear management + technical
success_examples.onfleet: Subtitle: "Engineering Manager | Mobile & Platform Development" - mobile is THE focus

failure_contrast.nextiva: "Engineering Leader | AI/ML Systems | Developer Experience" - three different value props dilute message
failure_contrast.shakepay: "Senior Software Engineer | Full-Stack Builder | Product Innovator" - generalist positioning for specialist role

factor.rule: If your subtitle has 2+ "pipes" (|) with unrelated skills, you're diluting your message

factor.name: First Bullet = Their #1 Need, Hands-On
factor.description: First bullet of current role demonstrates hands-on technical work in their exact top requirement

success_examples.lawdepot: They needed C#/.NET backend work - first bullet: "Architect backend services using C#, .NET 7+..."
success_examples.onfleet: They needed mobile expertise - summary led with "5+ years hands-on mobile", early bullet: "Architected cross-platform mobile-ready framework"

failure_contrast.nextiva: AI agents role, first bullet led with "Lead distributed team" instead of AI architecture work
failure_contrast.shakepay: Backend role, first bullet was generic "Build backend services" instead of specific fintech/crypto backend depth

factor.why_it_matters: Hiring managers scan first 10 seconds - first bullet determines if they keep reading

factor.name: Scannable Cover Letter Structure
factor.description: Visual structure that enables 30-second skim: bold headers OR very short paragraphs (3-5 sentences max)

success_examples.lawdepot: Bold section headers: "Proven Software Development Leadership", "Technical Excellence with C# & .NET Stack", etc.
success_examples.onfleet: 3 focused paragraphs, each 5-7 sentences with numbers embedded for easy scanning

failure_contrast.nextiva: Dense paragraphs: 9 sentences/180 words in paragraph 2, no visual breaks, overwhelming
failure_contrast.shakepay: 5 paragraphs but poorly structured, jumping topics, no clear narrative flow

factor.implementation: Either use bold section headers (corporate roles) OR limit paragraphs to 5 sentences max (startup roles)

factor.name: Recent Proof for Claimed Expertise
factor.description: If you claim expertise in area X, you must have 3+ recent (last 5 years) concrete examples in that exact domain

success_examples.onfleet: Claimed mobile expertise, backed with: 12+ apps shipped, 500k downloads, Swift/Kotlin code, App Store/Play deployments, Fastlane CI/CD
success_examples.lawdepot: Claimed C#/.NET expertise, backed with: current role using .NET 7+, Entity Framework Core, NUnit/xUnit, 90%+ code coverage

failure_contrast.nextiva: Claimed "AI/ML Systems" expertise - proof was 20-year-old M.Sc. thesis and 1 vague MCP integration

factor.litmus_test: If your proof is 5+ years old or based on academic work only, don't lead with that expertise claim

factor.name: Quality Engineering Signals
factor.description: Explicit mention of CI/CD, testing practices, code coverage, code reviews - signals professional engineering maturity

success_examples.lawdepot: "90%+ code coverage", "CI/CD pipelines", "Unit testing frameworks: NUnit, xUnit, MSTest"
success_examples.onfleet: "Fastlane/TestFlight", "CI/CD with automated testing", "pair programming and code reviews"

failure_contrast.shakepay: Mentioned "comprehensive test coverage" once but no specifics on frameworks, coverage metrics, or CI/CD

factor.why_it_works: Engineering managers want engineers who care about code quality - these specifics prove it

factor.name: Culture Matching in Tone and Language
factor.description: Cover letter tone and vocabulary mirrors target company's culture and values

success_examples.lawdepot: Corporate/formal: Bold section headers, business language, "growth", "innovation", structured approach
success_examples.onfleet: Startup/authentic: Warm tone, "psychological safety", "honest feedback", personal philosophy on leadership

how_to_apply.corporate: Use formal structure, business outcomes, scalability language
how_to_apply.startup: Use authentic voice, lean terminology ("eliminating waste"), personal examples

factor.note: Both successful apps matched company culture - but AFTER nailing technical fit first

factor.name: Geographic Alignment (When Applicable)
factor.description: For location-specific roles, emphasize local roots to reduce relocation risk concerns

success_example.lawdepot: Calgary company - highlighted U of Calgary degrees, Mount Royal experience, 5 earlier Calgary roles

factor.when_to_use: Local/hybrid roles in specific cities - shows commitment and reduces perceived risk

factor.name: Player-Coach Balance (Management Roles Only)
factor.description: For engineering manager roles, demonstrate both hands-on coding AND people leadership in parallel

success_examples.lawdepot: "Architect backend services using C#" (technical) + "Mentor team through 1:1s and performance reviews" (people)
success_examples.onfleet: "Architected cross-platform framework" (technical) + "Pair programming, code reviews, mentorship" (people)

factor.note: Only relevant for management roles - IC roles should emphasize technical depth over mentorship

resume_summary_formula.line_1: [X years] hands-on experience in [their exact #1 requirement]
resume_summary_formula.line_2: [Y years] leading teams + [scale indicator like team size, users, or systems managed]
resume_summary_formula.line_3: [Concrete achievement with number] relevant to their needs

resume_first_three_bullets.bullet_1: [Team leadership or architecture role] + [their exact tech stack] + [quantified delivery metric]
resume_first_three_bullets.bullet_2: [Specific technical work using their technologies] with [scale or complexity indicator]
resume_first_three_bullets.bullet_3: [Quality practices: CI/CD, testing, code reviews] + [specific metrics like coverage %]

cover_letter_formula.length: 1 page, 3-5 paragraphs OR sectioned with bold headers

paragraph_1.sentence_1: Hook connecting your expertise to their mission
paragraph_1.sentence_2: Your most relevant credential using their #1 requirement

body_structure.corporate_roles: Bold section headers for each qualification area with specific examples
body_structure.startup_roles: 2-3 narrative paragraphs, each with embedded numbers and specific technical examples

cover_letter_formula.closing: Express interest in discussing further + thank them

critical_mistakes_to_avoid.mistake: Multiple value propositions in subtitle (3 skills separated by |) - pick ONE that matches their top need
Claiming expertise in area where your proof is 5+ years old or only academic
First bullet leading with "Lead team" or management when role needs hands-on technical demonstration
Generic technical descriptions without specifics: scale, technologies, metrics, challenges
Dense cover letter paragraphs (8+ sentences or 100+ words) without visual breaks
Listing technologies without demonstrating depth in the specific ones they need
Academic or administrative experience taking 30%+ of resume for hands-on engineering role
Overly casual tone ("Sound familiar?", "You be the judge") that undermines technical authority for senior roles

application_checklist.check: Does my subtitle have ONE clear value prop matching their #1 need? (Not 3 different skills)
Do my first 3 resume bullets mention their exact tech stack by name?
Does my first bullet show hands-on work in their #1 requirement with specific tech and metrics?
For every expertise claim, do I have 3+ concrete, recent (last 5 years) proof points?
Does every major achievement have a specific number (%, count, time) attached?
Is my cover letter scannable in 30 seconds? (Bold headers OR 3-5 sentence paragraphs)
Did I mention specific quality practices (CI/CD, testing frameworks, code coverage %)?
Did I cut or compress experience 8+ years old that's not directly relevant?
Does my tone match their culture? (Corporate = formal structure; Startup = authentic voice)
For local roles, did I emphasize geographic connections?

before_you_submit.question: Can a hiring manager identify my core expertise in 5 seconds from my subtitle?
Do my first 3 bullets name-drop their exact technologies, not generic categories?
Would a skeptical reviewer believe my expertise claims based on recent proof?
Can someone scan my cover letter in 30 seconds and understand my value?
Have I cut generic statements that could apply to hundreds of other engineers?
